epigraph.text:
See, I am doing a new thing! Now it springs up; do you 
not perceive it?

content.text:
In Section [sec:tangent-velocity] we computed slopes of secant lines and 
watched them converge as the two points drew closer together. In 
Section [sec:limit-of-function] we gave that convergence a name: the 
*limit*. Now we take the final step. Armed with the full machinery of 
limits—limit laws, algebraic techniques, and even the rigorous 
$\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition—we return to the tangent problem and the 
velocity problem and solve them *completely*.

What emerges is the single most important concept in all of calculus: the 
**derivative**. It unifies the tangent problem and the velocity problem 
into one idea, and it opens the door to an extraordinary range of 
applications—from physics to economics, from biology to engineering. Every 
computation in this section will use the limit definition directly. This is 
deliberate. Before we earn shortcuts, we must understand what the shortcuts 
are shortcutting.
The Tangent Problem, Revisited

Recall the central question from Section [sec:tangent-velocity]: given a 
curve $y = f(x)$, what is the slope of the tangent line at a particular 
point? We answered this question informally by computing slopes of secant 
lines through $(a, f(a))$ and a nearby point $(a+h, f(a+h))$ and letting $h$ 
shrink toward zero. The slope of each secant line was
\[
 m_{\mathrm{sec}} = \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h},
\]
and we observed that these slopes seemed to converge to a single number as 
$h \to 0$. At the time, we lacked the formal tools to make this precise. 
Now we have them.

content.title: Slope of the Tangent Line
content.content:
The **tangent line** to the curve $y = f(x)$ at 
the point $P = (a, f(a))$ is the line through $P$ with slope
\[
 m = \limtozero{h} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h},
\]
provided this limit exists.

content.text:
The expression $\dfrac{f(a+h)-f(a)}{h}$ is called the **difference 
quotient**. It measures the average rate of change 
of $f$ over the interval from $a$ to $a+h$. The limit transforms this 
average into an *instantaneous* rate—the slope of the curve at the 
single point $x = a$.

content.title: Tangent line to a parabola
content.problem:
Find the equation of the tangent line to $f(x) = x^{2}$ at the point 
$(1, 1)$.
content.solution:
We compute the slope of the tangent line using the limit definition:
align*
 m &= h $\frac{f(1+h) - f(1)}{h}$ \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{(1+h)^{2} - 1}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{1 + 2h + h^{2} - 1}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{2h + h^{2}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{h(2 + h)}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} (2 + h) \\[6pt]
 &= 2.
\end{align*}

The tangent line passes through $(1, 1)$ with slope $m = 2$, so its 
equation in point-slope form is
\[
 y - 1 = 2(x - 1), \qquad\text{or equivalently}\qquad y = 2x - 1.
\]
In Section [sec:tangent-velocity], we guessed this answer by computing 
secant slopes and watching them approach $2$. Now we have *proved* it.

content.title: Tangent to a cubic
content.problem: Find the slope of the tangent line to $f(x) = x^{3}$ at $x = 2$.
content.solution:
We form the difference quotient and take the limit:
$$
 m &= \limtozero{h} \frac{f(2+h) - f(2)}{h} \__MATH_BLOCK_0__
Substituting:
\begin{align*}
 m &= \limtozero{h} \frac{8 + 12h + 6h^{2} + h^{3} - 8}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{12h + 6h^{2} + h^{3}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{h(12 + 6h + h^{2})}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} (12 + 6h + h^{2}) \\[6pt]
 &= 12.
$$
The tangent line to $y = x^{3}$ at $(2, 8)$ has slope $12$.

content.text: The Derivative at a Point

The limit we have been computing—the slope of the tangent line—appears in 
so many contexts and with such frequency that it deserves its own name.

content.title: The Derivative of $f$ at $a$
content.content:
The **derivative of $f$ at $a$**, denoted 
$f'(a)$ (read "$f$ prime of $a$"), is
\[
 f'(a) = \limtozero{h} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h},
\]
provided this limit exists. If $f'(a)$ exists, we say that $f$ is 
**differentiable** at $a$.

content.text:
There is an equivalent formulation that is sometimes more convenient. If we 
write $x = a + h$, then $h = x - a$ and $h \to 0$ corresponds to 
$x \to a$. The definition becomes:

Both forms define the same quantity. Use whichever is more natural for the 
problem at hand. When the function involves $x$ directly, the alternate form 
often avoids heavy algebra; when the function involves a composition, the 
$h$-form may be simpler.

content.title: Isaac Newton (1643--1727) and Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646--1716)
content.content:
The derivative was discovered independently by Isaac Newton in England and 
Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz in Germany during the 1660s and 1670s. Newton 
called derivatives *fluxions* and thought of them as instantaneous 
velocities of quantities "flowing" through time. Leibniz developed the 
$dy/dx$ notation and conceived of the derivative as a ratio of 
infinitesimal changes. Their approaches looked different but captured the 
same mathematical truth. The bitter priority dispute that followed 
overshadowed one of the most remarkable coincidences in intellectual 
history: two minds, working independently, arrived at the same 
revolutionary idea.

content.text: Computing Derivatives at a Point

Let us now put the definition to serious work. We compute $f'(a)$ for 
several fundamental functions, showing every algebraic step.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = x^{2}$ at $x = a$
content.problem: Find $f'(a)$ for $f(x) = x^{2}$ at an arbitrary point $x = a$.
content.solution:
$$
 f'(a) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{(a+h)^{2} - a^{2}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{a^{2} + 2ah + h^{2} - a^{2}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{2ah + h^{2}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{h(2a + h)}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} (2a + h) \\[6pt]
 &= 2a.
$$
For example, $f'(1) = 2$, $f'(3) = 6$, and $f'(-2) = -4$. The derivative 
of $x^{2}$ at any point $a$ is $2a$.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = x^{3}$ at $x = a$
content.solution:
Using the alternate form of the definition:
$$
 f'(a) &= \limto{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a} \__MATH_BLOCK_0__
Therefore:
\begin{align*}
 f'(a) &= \limto{x \to a} \frac{(x - a)(x^{2} + ax + a^{2})}{x - a} \\[6pt]
 &= \limto{x \to a} (x^{2} + ax + a^{2}) \\[6pt]
 &= a^{2} + a \cdot a + a^{2} \\[6pt]
 &= 3a^{2}.
$$
For example, $f'(2) = 3(4) = 12$, which confirms our earlier 
calculation in Example [ex:tangent-x3-at-2].

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ at $x = a$
content.solution:
$$
 f'(a) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h} \__MATH_BLOCK_0__
Substituting:
\begin{align*}
 f'(a) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{1}{h} \cdot \frac{-h}{a(a+h)} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{-1}{a(a+h)} \\[6pt]
 &= \frac{-1}{a \cdot a} \\[6pt]
 &= -\frac{1}{a^{2}}.
$$
The derivative of $\dfrac{1}{x}$ at $x = a$ is $-\dfrac{1}{a^{2}}$. Notice 
the negative sign: the function $\dfrac{1}{x}$ is *decreasing* for 
$x > 0$ (and also for $x < 0$), which is consistent with a negative 
derivative.

content.title: Derivative of $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ at $x = a$
content.solution:
$$
 f'(a) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{\sqrt{a+h} - \sqrt{a}}{h}.
$$
Direct substitution gives $\frac{0}{0}$, so we *rationalize the 
numerator* by multiplying by the conjugate:
$$
 f'(a) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{\sqrt{a+h} - \sqrt{a}}{h} \cdot 
 \frac{\sqrt{a+h} + \sqrt{a}}{\sqrt{a+h} + \sqrt{a}} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{(a+h) - a}{h\bigl(\sqrt{a+h} + \sqrt{a}\bigr)} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{h}{h\bigl(\sqrt{a+h} + \sqrt{a}\bigr)} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{1}{\sqrt{a+h} + \sqrt{a}} \\[6pt]
 &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{a} + \sqrt{a}} \\[6pt]
 &= \frac{1}{2\sqrt{a}}.
$$
For example, $f'(4) = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{4}} = \dfrac{1}{4}$ and 
$f'(9) = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{9}} = \dfrac{1}{6}$. As $a$ increases, the 
derivative decreases—the curve $y = \sqrt{x}$ flattens out as $x$ grows, 
which matches its graph.

content.text: Let us collect what we have found so far:

Do you notice a pattern? The derivative of $x^{n}$ at $a$ seems 
to be $na^{n-1}$—this works for $n = 2$, $n = 3$, $n = -1$ (since 
$\frac{1}{x} = x^{-1}$ and $-1 \cdot a^{-2} = -\frac{1}{a^{2}}$), and 
$n = \frac{1}{2}$ (since $\frac{1}{2}a^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{a}}$). 
We will return to this striking pattern in Section 3.4.
Instantaneous Velocity

In Section [sec:tangent-velocity] we studied an object moving along a 
straight line with position function $s(t)$. The average velocity over an 
interval $[a, a+h]$ was
\[
 v_{\mathrm{avg}} = \frac{s(a+h) - s(a)}{h}.
\]
The *instantaneous velocity* at time $t = a$ is the limit of these 
averages as the time interval shrinks to zero:

content.title: Instantaneous Velocity
content.content:
If $s(t)$ denotes the position of an object at time $t$, then the 
**instantaneous velocity** at $t = a$ is
\[
 v(a) = s'(a) = \limtozero{h} \frac{s(a+h) - s(a)}{h},
\]
provided this limit exists. The **speed** at $t = a$ is 
$\abs{v(a)}$.

content.text:
The derivative *is* the instantaneous velocity. This is not a metaphor 
or an approximation—it is an exact identity. The velocity of an object at a 
precise instant is the derivative of its position function at that instant.

content.title: Instantaneous velocity of a falling stone
content.problem:
A stone is dropped from a 400-foot cliff. Its height after $t$ seconds is 
$s(t) = 400 - 16t^{2}$. Find the instantaneous velocity at $t = 3$ seconds.
content.solution:
$$
 v(3) = s'(3) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{s(3+h) - s(3)}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{[400 - 16(3+h)^{2}] - [400 - 16(9)]}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{400 - 16(9 + 6h + h^{2}) - 400 + 144}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{-144 - 96h - 16h^{2} + 144}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{-96h - 16h^{2}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} (-96 - 16h) \\[6pt]
 &= -96.
$$
The instantaneous velocity at $t = 3$ is $-96$ ft/s. The negative sign 
indicates that the stone is moving *downward* (its height is 
decreasing). Its speed at that instant is $\abs{-96} = 96$ ft/s.

content.text: Rates of Change in Other Contexts

The tangent problem and the velocity problem are two faces of the same coin: 
both ask for an instantaneous rate of change. The derivative provides a 
unified answer.

content.title: Instantaneous Rate of Change
content.content:
The **instantaneous rate of change** 
of $f$ at $a$ is $f'(a)$. Equivalently, $f'(a)$ is the slope of the 
tangent line to $y = f(x)$ at $x = a$.

content.text:
The derivative can represent velocity, growth rate, marginal cost, reaction 
rate, or any quantity that measures how fast an output changes with respect 
to an input. The interpretation depends on the context; the mathematics is 
always the same.

content.title: Rate of change of area
content.problem:
A circular oil spill on still water has radius $r$ meters. Its area is 
$A(r) = \pi r^{2}$. Find the rate at which the area changes with respect 
to the radius when $r = 5$ meters.
content.solution:
$$
 A'(5) &= \limtozero{h} \frac{A(5+h) - A(5)}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{\pi(5+h)^{2} - 25\pi}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{\pi(25 + 10h + h^{2}) - 25\pi}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} \frac{10\pi h + \pi h^{2}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h} (10\pi + \pi h) \\[6pt]
 &= 10\pi.
$$
When the radius is 5 meters, the area is increasing at a rate of 
$10\pi \approx 31.4$ square meters per meter of radius. Each additional 
meter of radius adds roughly $31.4\,\mathrm{m}^{2}$ to the spill.

content.text: Tangent Lines and Normal Lines

We have defined the tangent line as the line through $(a, f(a))$ with slope 
$f'(a)$. The **normal line** at the same point is 
perpendicular to the tangent line.

content.title: Normal Line
content.content:
The **normal line** to $y = f(x)$ at $(a, f(a))$ is the line through 
$(a, f(a))$ perpendicular to the tangent line. If $f'(a) \neq 0$, the 
normal line has slope $-\dfrac{1}{f'(a)}$. If $f'(a) = 0$, the tangent 
line is horizontal and the normal line is the vertical line $x = a$.

content.title: Tangent and normal lines to $1/x$
content.problem:
Find the equations of the tangent line and the normal line to 
$f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ at the point $(2, \tfrac{1}{2})$.
content.solution:
From Example [ex:deriv-1overx-at-a], we know that 
$f'(a) = -\dfrac{1}{a^{2}}$, so
\[
 f'(2) = -\frac{1}{4}.
\]
The tangent line through $(2, \frac{1}{2})$ with slope $-\frac{1}{4}$ is
\[
 y - \tfrac{1}{2} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(x - 2),
 \qquad\text{or equivalently}\qquad
 y = -\tfrac{1}{4}x + 1.
\]
The normal line has slope $-\dfrac{1}{f'(2)} = -\dfrac{1}{-1/4} = 4$, so
\[
 y - \tfrac{1}{2} = 4(x - 2),
 \qquad\text{or equivalently}\qquad
 y = 4x - \tfrac{15}{2}.
\]

content.title: Tangent line to the square root function
content.problem: Find the equation of the tangent line to $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ at $(4, 2)$.
content.solution:
From Example [ex:deriv-sqrt-at-a], $f'(a) = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{a}}$, so
\[
 f'(4) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{4}} = \frac{1}{4}.
\]
The tangent line is
\[
 y - 2 = \tfrac{1}{4}(x - 4), \qquad\text{or}\qquad y = \tfrac{1}{4}x + 1.
\]

content.text: When Does the Derivative Fail to Exist?

The derivative $f'(a)$ is defined as a limit, and limits do not always exist. 
There are four common geometric situations in which a function fails to be 
differentiable at a point.

*1.\ Corner.** The function $f(x) = \abs{x}$ 
has a sharp corner at $x = 0$. The left-hand secant slopes approach $-1$ 
while the right-hand secant slopes approach $+1$:
\[
 \limto{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{\abs{0+h} - \abs{0}}{h} 
 = \limto{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{-h}{h} = -1,
 \qquad
 \limto{h \to 0^{+}} \frac{\abs{0+h} - \abs{0}}{h} 
 = \limto{h \to 0^{+}} \frac{h}{h} = 1.
\]
Since the one-sided limits disagree, $f'(0)$ does not exist.

*2.\ Cusp.** The function $f(x) = x^{2/3}$ has 
a cusp at $x = 0$. The difference quotient
\[
 \frac{f(0+h) - f(0)}{h} = \frac{h^{2/3}}{h} = \frac{1}{h^{1/3}}
\]
tends to $+\infty$ as $h \to 0^{+}$ and to $-\infty$ as $h \to 0^{-}$. The 
limit does not exist (even as an infinite limit), so $f'(0)$ is undefined. 
Geometrically, the tangent line would be vertical, but the curve approaches 
it from different sides.

*3.\ Vertical tangent.** The 
function $f(x) = x^{1/3}$ has a vertical tangent at $x = 0$:
\[
 \frac{f(0+h) - f(0)}{h} = \frac{h^{1/3}}{h} = \frac{1}{h^{2/3}} 
 \to +\infty \quad\text{as } h \to 0.
\]
The one-sided limits both tend to $+\infty$, so the tangent line is vertical: 
$x = 0$. Since the slope is not a real number, $f'(0)$ does not exist.

*4.\ Discontinuity.** If $f$ is not 
continuous at $a$, then $f$ is certainly not differentiable at $a$. (We will 
prove this in Section 3.2.) For example, the Heaviside step function 
$H(x) =

$ has a jump 
discontinuity at $x = 0$ and is not differentiable there.

content.title: Differentiable is ``stronger'' than continuous
content.content:
Every differentiable function is continuous (Theorem [thm:diff-implies-cont] 
in Section 3.2), but a continuous function need not be differentiable. The 
function $f(x) = \abs{x}$ is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at 
$x = 0$.

content.title: Where is $f(x) = \abs{x - 3}$ not differentiable?
content.problem: Determine where $f(x) = \abs{x - 3}$ fails to be differentiable.
content.solution:
We can write
\[
 f(x) = \begin{cases} -(x-3) = 3 - x & \text{if } x < 3, \\
 x - 3 & \text{if } x \ge 3. \end{cases}
\]
For $x \neq 3$, the function is a linear function on each piece, so it is 
differentiable there. At $x = 3$:
\[
 \limto{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{f(3+h) - f(3)}{h} 
 = \limto{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{\abs{h}}{h} 
 = \limto{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{-h}{h} = -1,
\]
\[
 \limto{h \to 0^{+}} \frac{f(3+h) - f(3)}{h} 
 = \limto{h \to 0^{+}} \frac{h}{h} = 1.
\]
Since $-1 \neq 1$, the limit does not exist and $f'(3)$ does not exist. 
The function has a corner at $x = 3$.

content.title: Finding the Derivative at a Point
content.content:
To compute $f'(a)$:

1. Write the difference quotient $\dfrac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h}$.

2. Simplify the expression algebraically (expand, factor, rationalize, 
 combine fractions) until you can cancel the $h$ in the denominator.

3. Evaluate $\limtozero{h}$ of the simplified expression.
*Alternatively*, use the form $\dfrac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a}$ and take 
$\limto{x \to a}$. Choose whichever form leads to simpler algebra.

content.title: A derivative that does not exist
content.problem: Show that $f(x) = (x - 1)^{2/3}$ is not differentiable at $x = 1$.
content.solution:
$$
 \frac{f(1+h) - f(1)}{h} 
 &= \frac{(1+h-1)^{2/3} - 0}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \frac{h^{2/3}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \frac{1}{h^{1/3}}.
$$
As $h \to 0^{+}$, $h^{1/3} \to 0^{+}$, so $1/h^{1/3} \to +\infty$. 
As $h \to 0^{-}$, $h^{1/3} \to 0^{-}$, so $1/h^{1/3} \to -\infty$. 
The limit does not exist, and $f'(1)$ is undefined. The graph has a cusp 
at $(1, 0)$.

content.caption: Secant lines through $P$ approach the tangent line as $Q$ moves toward $P$.
content.alt: A coordinate plane showing a parabola y equals x squared in teal. Point P is marked at coordinates one, one. Three secant lines are drawn from P to points Q1, Q2, and Q3, progressively closer to P. The secant lines become darker gray as the second point approaches P. A tangent line in orange passes through P with slope 2, representing the limit of the secant lines as Q approaches P.

content.caption: The difference quotient measures the slope of the secant line.
content.alt: A coordinate plane showing a smooth increasing curve with two points marked: A at coordinates a, f of a and B at coordinates a plus h, f of a plus h. A secant line connects the two points. A right triangle is formed beneath the secant line with horizontal leg labeled h representing the change in x, and vertical leg labeled f of a plus h minus f of a representing the change in y. The slope of the secant line equals the difference quotient: f of a plus h minus f of a, all divided by h.

content.caption: The velocity $v(t) = s'(t)$ is the derivative of position. At $t = 3$, the tangent line to $s$ has slope $-96$, which equals $v(3)$.
content.alt: Two vertically stacked coordinate planes sharing a horizontal time axis from zero to five seconds. The top panel shows the position function s of t equals 400 minus 16 t squared, a downward-opening parabola starting at height 400 feet at t equals zero and reaching zero at t equals five. A tangent line in orange is drawn at t equals three with slope negative 96. The bottom panel shows the velocity function v of t equals negative 32 t, a straight line starting at zero and decreasing linearly. At t equals three, the velocity is negative 96 feet per second, indicated by a point and horizontal dashed line. A vertical dashed line at t equals three connects both panels, showing that velocity is the derivative of position.

content.caption: Four ways a function can fail to be differentiable at a point.
content.alt: Four coordinate planes arranged in a two-by-two grid, each showing a function that is not differentiable at x equals zero. Panel a shows the absolute value function y equals absolute value of x, forming a sharp V-shaped corner at the origin. Panel b shows y equals x to the two-thirds power with a cusp pointing downward at the origin. Panel c shows y equals the cube root of x with a vertical tangent line at the origin, indicated by a dashed vertical line. Panel d shows the Heaviside step function with a jump discontinuity at x equals zero, jumping from y equals zero to y equals one.

marginNotes.text:
For the word of God is living and active, 
sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing to the division of soul and 
of spirit, of joints and of marrow, and discerning the thoughts and 
intentions of the heart.

How precious to me are your thoughts, 
O God! How vast is the sum of them! If I would count them, they are more 
than the sand.

The race is not to the swift, nor the 
battle to the strong, but time and chance happen to them all.

Give careful thought to the paths 
for your feet and be steadfast in all your ways. Do not turn to the 
right or the left.

marginNotes.author: Isaac Newton
marginNotes.text:
If I have seen further, it is by standing on 
the shoulders of giants.

marginNotes.author: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz
marginNotes.text:
It is unworthy of excellent men 
to lose hours like slaves in the labor of calculation which could safely 
be relegated to anyone else if machines were used.

marginNotes.author: Blaise Pascal
marginNotes.text:
The heart has its reasons of which reason 
knows nothing.
